stops.name: Puerta del Sol
stops.shortDescription: A bustling semi-circular square marking the center of the Spanish radial road network (Kilometer Zero). It features the famous clock whose bells mark the traditional eating of twelve grapes on New Year's Eve.

stops.name: Plaza Mayor
stops.shortDescription: A grand Renaissance square completely enclosed by residential buildings with 237 balconies facing inward. Historically, it hosted markets, bullfights, soccer games, and even public executions.

stops.name: Mercado de San Miguel
stops.shortDescription: A covered market built in 1916 famous for its elegant wrought-iron architecture. It is now a vibrant culinary center serving high-quality Spanish tapas, oysters, and wines.

stops.name: Plaza de la Villa
stops.shortDescription: One of Madrid's best-preserved historical monuments, bordered by narrow streets. The buildings here represent the city's main architectural styles from the 15th, 16th, and 17th centuries.

stops.name: Almudena Cathedral
stops.shortDescription: Madrid's main Catholic cathedral, consecrated by Pope John Paul II in 1993. Unusually for European cathedrals, its interior is distinctly modern, featuring pop-art style decor and brightly colored chapels.

stops.name: Royal Palace of Madrid
stops.shortDescription: The official residence of the Spanish royal family, though now only used for state ceremonies. With over 3,400 rooms, it is the largest functioning royal palace and largest by floor area in Europe.

stops.name: Plaza de Oriente
stops.shortDescription: A monumental square facing the Royal Palace, featuring beautifully manicured gardens and a collection of limestone statues depicting historical Spanish kings.

stops.name: Teatro Real
stops.shortDescription: Madrid's major opera house located right on Plaza de Oriente. Since its opening in 1850, it has been one of the great theaters of Europe.

stops.name: Sabatini Gardens
stops.shortDescription: Elegant, neoclassical gardens situated on the north side of the Royal Palace. They offer a tranquil retreat with geometric hedges, pools, and statues.

stops.name: Plaza de España
stops.shortDescription: A large, popular square featuring a prominent monument to Miguel de Cervantes, the author of Don Quixote, situated at the western end of Gran Vía.

stops.name: Temple of Debod
stops.shortDescription: An authentic ancient Egyptian temple dismantled and rebuilt in Madrid. It was gifted to Spain in 1968 and offers one of the most stunning sunset views in the city.

stops.name: Gran Vía
stops.shortDescription: Often called the 'Spanish Broadway,' this bustling street is famous for its grandiose 20th-century architecture, massive theaters, and endless shopping.

stops.name: Plaza del Callao
stops.shortDescription: The bustling, cinematic heart of Gran Vía, surrounded by historic movie theaters and massive digital billboards reminiscent of Times Square or Piccadilly Circus.

stops.name: Monasterio de las Descalzas Reales
stops.shortDescription: A serene royal monastery founded in 1559. It is surprisingly wealthy, housing incredible tapestries and artwork donated by the noblewomen who lived there in seclusion.
